personal_profile.name: Margaret Chen

spouse.name: Robert Chen
spouse.health_notes: Heart condition — had a scare in Feb 2026

personal_profile.career: Retired high school English teacher (35 years, Portland Public Schools)
personal_profile.tech_details: Uses iPad daily, comfortable with email and video calls, learning to use banking apps
personal_profile.communication_preference: Warm, patient, uses analogies. Prefers bullet points. Best engagement on Tuesday mornings.

risk_profile.biggest_fear: Outliving savings — especially with Robert's health concerns
risk_profile.biggest_goal: Travel while healthy enough to enjoy it
risk_profile.recent_shift: More conservative-leaning after Robert's medical scare (March 2026)

travel.dreams: Visit grandchildren in Seattle monthly, annual trip to Europe
travel.specifics: Wants to take Lisa's kids to Italy — where she studied abroad in college

healthcare.ltc_insurance: None — exploring options. Escalated to James Rodriguez for guidance.

housing.current_plan: Stay in current home 5-10 years, then consider downsizing
housing.future_considerations: Has discussed a retirement community with Robert but not committed

legacy.education_fund: Exploring 529 options for grandchildren — wants to contribute $500/year per grandchild
legacy.estate_planning: Has basic will, needs to update beneficiaries

conversation_history.summary: Completed full life-plan interview. Margaret was open and engaged. Expressed anxiety about healthcare costs, especially with Robert's health. Excited about Europe trip planning. Agent noted: respond with reassurance on longevity concerns. She lit up talking about her grandchildren.

Margaret asked about 401(k) withdrawal strategies. Discussed RMD timeline (starts at 73 under SECURE 2.0). Agent provided educational comparison of systematic withdrawal vs. bucket strategy vs. percentage-based approaches. Margaret was engaged but confused RMDs with early withdrawal penalties — need to simplify language on this topic in future.

flags.description: Confused RMDs with early withdrawal penalties. Simplify language on tax-related withdrawal topics.

conversation_history.summary: Robert had a medical scare earlier in the week — chest pains, ER visit, turned out to be non-cardiac but stressful. Margaret was visibly worried about costs and what would happen if Robert needed long-term care. Agent pivoted to emotional support first, validated her fears, then gently discussed emergency fund adequacy ($45k covers ~10 months of expenses). Escalated LTC insurance question to human advisor James Rodriguez. Updated risk profile to more conservative leaning.

flags.description: LTC insurance exploration — escalated to James Rodriguez
Margaret very anxious about Robert's health and financial implications

agent_notes: Uses gardening metaphors — she responds well to them ('think of your portfolio like a garden — some plants grow fast, others are slow and steady')
Prefers bullet points over long paragraphs
Husband Robert sometimes joins calls — when he does, he's more analytical and asks pointed questions. Adjust for two-person conversation.
Gets anxious when discussing healthcare costs — lead with reassurance, then facts
Loves talking about her grandchildren — good way to transition into legacy planning topics
After Session 2: avoid the term 'Required Minimum Distribution' without immediately explaining it in plain language
